request.question: Which artist did Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller appear with that liked showering himself (and others) with confetti?

title: Ichthyosis with confetti
Tony Hiller
KDrew
Residential water use in the U.S. and Canada
List of most disliked YouTube videos

sentences: Ichthyosis en confetti, also known as ichthyosis with confetti, congenital reticular ichthyosiform erythroderma (CRIE) and ichthyosis variegata, is a very rare form of congenital ichthyosis in which healthy patches of normal skin co-exist within the abnormal skin areas.

The condition is caused by a frameshift mutation in the keratin 10 gene (KRT10); mutant keratin 10 accumulates in the nucleolus, a sub-nuclear structure, rather than within cellular intermedite filaments like the wild-type protein.
Children with the condition exhibit red, flaky skin; however, for reasons not yet totally clear, wild type clonal patches of skin start to appear, in place of the red, flaky skin.
Due to the clonal nature of the growth of the normal skin cells, it appears the patient is covered with confetti, hence the name of the condition.
It has been hypothesized that this is the result of a combination of mitotic recombination and natural selection within the skin.
Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927) is a British songwriter.
He began his musical career as a member of the song and dance duo The Hiller Brothers, sharing the stage with his brother Irving.

The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.
Kevin Nicholas Drew, professionally and better known as KDrew, is an artist, producer and DJ from New Jersey.
KDrew produces primarily EDM and is perhaps best known for his remixes for Zedd and Lady Gaga, although his original records "Bullseye" and Circles have generated millions of plays online.
The Boston Celtics of the NBA currently use "Circles" as the intro music for their home games at TD Garden.
KDrew has also done official remixes for Nelly Furtado, Adventure Club, T.I. and others and collaborated with Taryn Manning on "Summer Ashes", which was released on July 23, 2013.
On February 5, 2015 MTV premiered KDrew's music video for "Let Me Go".
KDrew performed at Global Dance Festival at Red Rocks Amphitheatre.
He co-wrote the song "I Want You to Know", by Zedd featuring Selena Gomez, alongside Zedd and Ryan Tedder.
KDrew also features as an additional producer on the tracks "Done With Love" and "Straight Into the Fire" off Zedd's "True Colors" album.
KDrew co-produced two songs on Hilary Duff's Breathe In.
Breathe Out.
album, "Confetti" and "Arms Around a Memory".
Residential water use (also called domestic use, household use, or tap water use) includes all indoor and outdoor uses of drinking quality water at single-family and multifamily dwellings.

These uses include a number of defined purposes (or water end uses) such as flushing toilets, washing clothes and dishes, showering and bathing, drinking, food preparation, watering lawns and gardens, and maintaining swimming pools.
Some of these end uses are detectable (and measurable) while others are more difficult to gauge.
This list of most disliked YouTube videos contains the top 40 videos with the most dislikes of all time, as derived from YouTube charts.
The dislike count is taken directly from the page of the video itself.
YouTube implemented a like and dislike button on these pages in 2010, part of a major redesign of the site.

This served as a replacement for their five-star rating system; YouTube's designers found the previous system ineffective because the options to rate a video between two and four stars were rarely selected.
Of the 40 videos in this list, 14 also appear in the list of most viewed YouTube videos and 10 appear in the list of most liked YouTube videos.
Coleonema pulchellum, commonly known as confetti bush, buchu, diosma or breath of heaven, is a shrub which is endemic to South Africa.
It grows to between 0.8 and 1 metre high.
Single pink flowers to about 8 mm in diameter appear between May and October in the species native range.
Toby Love is the self-titled debut studio album by Puerto Rican-American performer Toby Love.
It was released on September 5, 2006 by Sony BMG Norte.
The album featured collaborations with Puerto Rican rapper Voltio and Puerto Rican duo R.K.M & Ken-Y, among others.

Upon release, the album debuted at number twenty-seven on the "Billboard" Latin Albums chart, number forty-two on the "Billboard" Heatseekers Albums chart, number three on the Mid-Atlantic area division of the "Billboard" Heatseekers Albums chart and number eleven on "Billboard" Latin Rhythm Albums chart.

The album spawned three singles, with the lead single, "Tengo Un Amor", becoming a success, in the Latin market, reaching number one in the "Billboard" Latin Rhythm Airplay chart, the top three of both the "Billboard" Latin Songs and "Billboard" Tropical Songs charts, while peaking at number 100 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in the United States.
"Toby Love" was awarded a "Billboard" Latin Music Award for "R&B/Hip-Hop Album of the Year".
This list of most liked YouTube videos contains the top 40 videos with the most likes of all time, as derived from YouTube charts.
The like count is taken directly from the page of the video itself.
YouTube implemented a like and dislike button on these pages in March 2010, part of a major redesign of the site.

This served as a replacement for their five-star rating system; YouTube's designers found the previous system ineffective because the options to rate a video between two and four stars were rarely selected.
Of the 40 videos in this list, 37 also appear in the list of most viewed YouTube videos and 15 appear in the list of most disliked YouTube videos.
All videos in this list are music videos.

"There's No Place Like London" is a song co-written by Lynsey de Paul and Gerard Kenny, that was first recorded by Shirley Bassey with a 54 piece orchestra, produced by de Paul and released as a single in 1986.

She performed it in 1986 as part of her “Live from the Piccadilly” show as well as on the 1987 Royal Variety Performance and ironically for her "Shirley Bassey live" in Berlin in honour of 750 years of Berlin in 1987.
It was released for the first time on CD to tie-in with the UK held Olympic Games in 2012 on the album, "Olympic Tracks".
It is listed in the BBC music database which catalogues songs they play.
Speaking about the recording of the song in an interview, de Paul said "we wrote.
"There's No Place Like London", and the Tourist Board liked it, and then we thought of an artist and I thought of Shirley Bassey.
So I approached her and asked her to sing it and she really liked and that's the way round it came, it didn't come that I wrote for her I just had the song and said would you sing it.
And she was great."
It is still one of the most played Bassey songs on BBC radio.
Two different live Bassey performances of the song appear on the Odeon Entertainment 2010 DVD, "Shirley Bassey On TV".
Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr. (born January 13, 1935) is an American actor and comedian.
He is known for his exuberance and flamboyant personality, including his wild moustache and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Which artist did Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller appear with that liked showering himself (and others) with confetti?
hop1_docs: Tony Hiller | Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927) is a British songwriter.  He began his musical career as a member of the song and dance duo The Hiller Brothers, sharing the stage with his brother Irving.  The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.

Rip Taylor | Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr. (born January 13, 1935) is an American actor and comedian.  He is known for his exuberance and flamboyant personality, including his wild moustache and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.

Ichthyosis with confetti | Ichthyosis en confetti, also known as ichthyosis with confetti, congenital reticular ichthyosiform erythroderma (CRIE) and ichthyosis variegata, is a very rare form of congenital ichthyosis in which healthy patches of normal skin co-exist within the abnormal skin areas.  The condition is caused by a frameshift mutation in the keratin 10 gene (KRT10); mutant keratin 10 accumulates in the nucleolus, a sub-nuclear structure, rather than within cellular intermedite filaments like the wild-type protein.  Children with the condition exhibit red, flaky skin; however, for reasons not yet totally clear, wild type clonal patches of skin start to appear, in place of the red, flaky skin.  Due to the clonal nature of the growth of the normal skin cells, it appears the patient is covered with confetti, hence the name of the condition.  It has been hypothesized that this is the result of a combination of mitotic recombination and natural selection within the skin.

Tony Booth (artist) | Anthony "Tony" Booth (22 June 1933 – 11 January 2017) was a British commercial artist best known as the original poster artist for The Beatles and other bands in the Merseybeat era.  He was known around Liverpool in the early 1960s as Brian Epstein's "right-hand man".

Scurfield | The family name Scurfield appears to be of Old Norse, or Viking origin.  “Scur” in Old English is “shower” as in rain.  In Old Norse “skur” also carries the meaning shower, as in shower of rain, or a shower of arrows.  The most likely origin of the surname Scurfield, therefore, is the name of an ancient field of battle.

Tony Reali | Anthony Joseph Paul "Tony" Reali (born July 4, 1978) is an American sports personality and television host of "Around the Horn" on ESPN.  In April 2014 it was announced he would be a contributor on social media for "Good Morning America", which he began on October 8, 2014 but has not appeared in 2015, other than a couple sports related segments introduced as "ESPN's Tony Reali".  He was "Stat Boy" on "Pardon the Interruption" from the show's debut in 2001 until late 2014.

The Careless Years | The Careless Years is a 1957 film from United Artists directed by Arthur Hiller and produced by Edward Lewis.  The film was the directorial debut for Hiller.  The film stars Dean Stockwell and Natalie Trundy in an early film appearance.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller — [1]: "Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927)"
- Rip Taylor — [1]: "Rip Taylor"
- Rip Taylor — [2]: "showering himself (and others) with confetti."

Ambiguities:
- None; "Rip Taylor" appears unambiguously in [1] and [2].

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing facts. Passages show Tony Hiller appeared with Rip Taylor, who "showered himself (and others) with confetti." Final answer: Rip Taylor.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "Rip Taylor | Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr." for full biography.
- Open page "Tony Hiller | Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927)" to verify the appearance list.
- (Optional) Search query: "Rip Taylor confetti habit" for corroborating sources.
hop2_docs: Tony Hiller | Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller (born 30 July 1927) is a British songwriter.  He began his musical career as a member of the song and dance duo The Hiller Brothers, sharing the stage with his brother Irving.  The Hiller Brothers appeared with many artists of the time including Alma Cogan, Tommy Cooper, Val Doonican, Matt Monro, The Shadows, Bernard Manning, Kathy Kirby, Roger Whittaker, Rip Taylor, Gene Vincent, Lance Percival, Tessie O'Shea, Frank Ifield, Deep River Boys, The Dallas Boys, Clark Brothers, Paul Melba and Ray Burns.

Rip Taylor | Charles Elmer "Rip" Taylor, Jr. (born January 13, 1935) is an American actor and comedian.  He is known for his exuberance and flamboyant personality, including his wild moustache and his habit of showering himself (and others) with confetti.

Nightwing (film) | Nightwing is a 1979 American horror film directed by Arthur Hiller.  The screenplay by Martin Cruz Smith, Steve Shagan, and Bud Shrake is based on the 1977 novel of the same title by Smith.  Its tagline is "Day belongs to man, but night is theirs!"  It was one of many "Jaws" rip-offs that were popular in the late 1970s and early 1980s, including "" (1977), "Tentacles" (1977), "The Pack" (1977), "Piranha" (1978), "Alligator" (1980) and "Great White" (1980).  It also was Hiller's only horror film.

Drainie-Taylor Biography Prize | The Drainie-Taylor Biography Prize was a Canadian literary award, presented by the Writers' Trust of Canada to a work judged as the year's best work of biography, autobiography or personal memoir by a Canadian writer.
Tony Taylor (basketball) | Tony Taylor (born August 9, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for Banvit of the Turkish Basketball Super League (BSL).

D. J. Taylor | David John Taylor (born 1960) is a British critic, novelist and biographer.  After attending school in Norwich, he read Modern History at St John's College, Oxford, and has received the 2003 Whitbread Biography Award for his biography of George Orwell.  His novel "Derby Day" was longlisted for the 2011 Man Booker Prize.  Previously a member of Norwich Writers' Circle.

Backbeat (biography) | Backbeat: Earl Palmer's Story is the biography of pioneer rock and roll drummer Earl Palmer.  The book is by music journalist Tony Scherman with a foreword by Wynton Marsalis.  More than half the text is directly quoted from Palmer, making the book as much an autobiography as it is a biography.
response.summary_2: Anthony Toby "Tony" Hiller appeared with Rip Taylor, who was known for showering himself (and others) with confetti.
